text:
Silencing of Hepatic AATF Ameliorates Metabolic Dysfunction Associated Steatohepatitis by Promoting Fatty Acid β-Oxidation in Experimental models

Akshatha N. Srinivas 1 , Diwakar Suresh 1 , Manju Moorthy 2 , Gopalkrishna Ramaswamy 2 ,  Prasanna  Kumar  Santhekadur 1 ,  Deepak  Suvarna 3 ,  and  Divya  P. Kumar 1 *

1 Department of Biochemistry, CEMR, JSS Medical College, JSS Academy of Higher Education and Research, Mysuru, Karnataka, India

2 TheraCUES Innovations Pvt. Ltd, Bangalore, India

3 Department of Gastroenterology, JSS Medical College and Hospital, JSS Academy of Higher Education and Research, Mysuru- 570015, Karnataka, India

* Correspondence:

Dr. Divya P. Kumar Associate Professor Department of Biochemistry JSS Medical College, JSS AHER SS Nagar, Mysuru- 570015 Karnataka, India

divyapkumar@jssuni.edu.in

ABSTRACT

Background: Metabolic  dysfunction-associated  steatotic  liver  disease  (MASLD) affects 30% of the global population and has emerged as a significant public health concern.  It  is  a  multifactorial  pathological  condition  driven  by  various  molecular drivers.  Thus,  identifying  and  understanding  the  molecular  drivers  involved  in MASLD pathogenesis is essential for developing targeted therapeutic strategies.

Aim: To elucidate the role of apoptosis antagonizing transcription factor (AATF) as a molecular  driver  in  the  pathogenesis  of  MASLD  and  its  underlying  mechanisms. Methods: A preclinical murine model resembling human MASLD was employed, in which C57Bl/6 mice were fed either a chow diet with normal water (CD) or a western diet with sugar water (WD). After 12 weeks, mice were injected via the tail vein with AAV8 serotype particles and randomized into four groups: (i) CD with AAV8-TBGNull,  (ii)  CD  with  AAV8-TBG-siAATF,  (iii)  WD  with  AAV8-TBG-Null  (WD control),  and  (iv)  WD  with  AAV8-TBG-siAATF  (WD  siAATF). Biochemical, histological,  and  molecular  analyses  were  performed  using  serum  and  liver  tissues. Untargeted  metabolomics  was  carried  out  in  the  liver  tissues  of  the  experimental MASLD.

Results: AATF was upregulated in cellular and preclinical models of MASLD. Liverspecific silencing of AATF reduced body weight, liver weight, and insulin resistance in WD mice compared to WD controls but had no effect on CD mice. Biochemical, histological, and molecular analyses showed reduced liver injury, steatosis, inflammation,  and  fibrosis  in  AATF-silenced  WD  mice.  Furthermore,  untargeted metabolomics revealed increased levels of glycerophospholipids, such as phosphatidylcholines,  phosphatidylserines,  and  phosphatidylethanolamines,  in  the WD siAATF mice. Consistently, expression of hepatic carnitine palmitoyl transferase (CPT1)  and  peroxisome  proliferator-activated  receptor  alpha/gamma  (PPAR  α/γ), which  promote  fatty  acid  beta  oxidation,  was  upregulated  in  the  WD  siAATF compared to WD controls.

Conclusion: These findings show a molecular link between AATF and hepatic lipid metabolism,  implying  that  AATF  could  be  a  promising  therapeutic  target  for MASLD.

KEYWORDS

Apoptosis antagonizing transcription factor, metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease, adeno-associated virus 8, fatty acid β-oxidation, metabolomics

INTRODUCTION

Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver  disease (MASLD), formerly  termed non-alcoholic  fatty  liver  disease  (NAFLD),  stands  as  the  prevailing  chronic  liver ailment impacting more than 30% of the adult population worldwide (1,2). It notably afflicts individuals with obesity and  type  2 diabetes,  emerging  as  a  primary contributor  to  advanced  liver  disease  and  hepatocellular  carcinoma  (HCC)  (3,4). MASLD encompasses a spectrum of advancing  steatotic liver  conditions,  spanning from benign metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver (MAFL) to steatohepatitis (MASH), presenting with varying degrees of liver fibrosis that could advance to  cirrhosis  (5).  MASLD  not  only  heightens  the  likelihood  of  liver-related complications such as cirrhosis, end-stage liver disease, and hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)  but  also  escalates  the  risk  of  multiple  extraneous  manifestations,  including cardiovascular disease (CVD), chronic kidney disease (CKD), and specific forms of extrahepatic  cancers  (6). As  MASLD progresses  to  the  end  stage  of  liver  disease, treatment options become increasingly limited, often leading to consideration for liver transplantation (7). Early detection, and intervention, are therefore paramount. While lifestyle modifications remain fundamental to managing the condition, pharmacological treatments can complement these interventions (8). Despite numerous  clinical  studies,  the  pathogenesis  of  MASLD  remains  elusive,  and  the mechanisms underlying its occurrence and progression are not yet fully  understood (9). Therefore, elucidating the molecular pathways involved in MASLD pathogenesis is crucial for identifying potential therapeutic targets.

MASLD  manifests  with  the  accumulation  of  lipids  in  the  liver,  stemming  from disturbances in both lipid uptake (from circulating sources or de novo synthesis) and lipid  disposal  (via  fatty  acid  oxidation  or  secretion  of  triglyceride-rich  lipoproteins) (10).  This  disruption  results  in  the  buildup  of  lipotoxic  compounds,  cellular  stress, injury,  and  fibrosis  (10,11).  The  regulation  of  lipid  metabolism  is  complex  and multifaceted, involving various factors like membrane transporters, metabolic enzymes,  and  transcriptional  regulators  (12).  Glycerophospholipids,  a  subclass  of phospholipids that are the major constituents of cell membranes, play a key role in lipid  metabolism,  and  alterations  in  the  composition  of  glycerophospholipids  have been reported in individuals  with  MASLD  (13,14).  Phosphatidylcholine  (PC), phosphatidylethanolamine  (PE),  phosphatidylserine  (PS),  phosphatidylinositol  (PI), and cardiolipin (CL) are a diverse array of glycerophospholipids that are well studied for  their  role  in  cell  signaling  and  lipid  metabolism  (14).  Glycerophospholipids  are involved in inflammatory, oxidative stress, and bile acid metabolism pathways, which are key drivers in the disease pathogenesis of MASLD. Furthermore, glycerophospholipids, in addition to contributing to the integrity of the mitochondrial membrane, also play a role in the beta-oxidation of fatty acids (15). It is observed that, in MASLD patients, reduced fatty acid β-oxidation in the liver results in insufficient counteraction  to  the  lipotoxic  effects  induced  by  harmful  lipids  (15).  Studies  have shown that peroxisome proliferator-activated receptors alpha/gamma (PPAR-α/γ) and carnitine  palmitoyltransferase-1  alpha  (CPT-1α),  major  regulators  of  fatty  acid  βoxidation,  are  downregulated  in  MASLD  (16).  Interestingly,  activation  of  PPARγmediated  autophagy  via  the  AMPK/mTOR  pathway  led  to  decreased  steatosis, increased rates of fatty acid β-oxidation, and ultimately downregulated hepatic lipid degeneration  in  obese  mice  (17).  Recent  studies  have  demonstrated  the  efficacy  of CPT1α gene therapy aimed at enhancing fatty acid β-oxidation in the liver, resulting in  a  reduction  of  steatosis  in  mice  (18).  Collectively,  these  studies  suggest  that patients with MASLD exhibit decreased fatty acid β-oxidation, and rejuvenating this process could represent a promising avenue for MASLD treatment.

Apoptosis  antagonizing  transcription  factor  (AATF)  is  a  multifaceted  transcription factor  recognized  for  its  pivotal  involvement  in  essential  cellular  functions  (19).  It serves as a dynamic regulator of fundamental cellular processes such as transcriptional control, cell cycle and apoptosis regulation, ribosome biogenesis, and response to cellular stress (19) AATF protects cells from oxidative stress, ER stress, DNA damage, and hypoxia by modulating the cell cycle, autophagy, and apoptosis (20). The widely studied role of AATF is apoptosis regulation exerted by activation of antiapoptotic and inhibition of proapoptotic pathways (21). Pathologically, AATF has been  implicated  in  numerous  cancer  types,  including  leukemia,  breast  cancer, neuroblastoma,  lung  cancer,  and  colon  cancer  (22-24).  Our  previous  investigations have revealed elevated levels of hepatic AATF in HCC, where it plays a significant role  in  promoting  tumor  angiogenesis  (25).  Studies  have  also  demonstrated  the inhibitory  effects  of  curcumin  on  MASH-HCC  by  downregulating  AATF  via  the KLF4-SP1  pathway  (26).  We  recently  explored  the  involvement  of  the  SIRT1TIMP3-TACE axis in AATF upregulation, with subsequent TACE inhibition leading to  AATF  downregulation  and  attenuation  of  MASH-associated  liver  tumorigenesis (27).  However,  the  mechanistic  role  of  AATF  in  the  pathophysiology  of  MASH remains poorly understood.

In  this  study,  we  examined  the  novel  role  of  AATF  in  diet-induced  experimental MASH.  We  found  that  AATF  expression  positively  correlated  with  the  MASLD spectrum, and liver-specific knockdown of AATF significantly improved MASH by reducing steatosis, inflammation, and fibrosis. Additionally, we investigated the effect of  hepatic  silencing  of  AATF  on  MASH  through  untargeted  metabolomics.  In  the current study, we integrated biochemical and molecular parameters and metabolomics to provide novel insights into the role of AATF in hepatic metabolism. These findings collectively shed light on the unexplored mechanistic role of AATF in regulating lipid metabolism.

MATERIALS AND METHODS

Materials

Customized  chow  diet  and  western  diet  were  purchased  from  Research  Diets  Inc. (USA). Biochemical  assay kits  were  from  Monlab  tests  (Spain).  Sodium  Palmitate, Sodium  Oleate,  Oil  Red  O  solution,  Liberase,  antibody  to  AATF,  TRIzol  reagent, RIPA  buffer,  and  Picrosirius  red  stain  were  from  Sigma-Aldrich  (USA).  FBS, glutamine, collagen, penicillin-streptomycin solution, verso cDNA synthesis kit, and DyNamo Colorflash SYBR green kit from Thermo Fisher Scientific (USA). Primers were from Integrated DNA Technologies (IDT). Western blotting materials from BioRad  and  western  bright  ECL  HRP  substrate.  Cell  culture  media,  supplements, glucose,  fructose,  cell  strainer,  and  hematoxylin  were  from  HiMedia  Laboratories. Antibodies to pJNK, JNK, pErk1/2, and Erk1/2 were from Santa Cruz Biotechnology; antibodies to F4/80, Desmin, and β-actin were from Cell Signaling Technologies.

Animal study

Male  C57Bl/6NCrl  mice  aged  6  weeks  procured  from  Hylasco  Biotechnology  Pvt. Ltd.  (Charles  River  Laboratories,  Hyderabad,  Telangana,  India)  were  used  for  the study. All the mice were accommodated in the central animal housing facility at the Center for Experimental Pharmacology and Toxicology, JSS AHER following a 12hour light-dark cycle, consistent with previously established protocols (28). Following one week of acclimatization,  study  protocols  were  commenced.    All  animal-related procedures adhered to the guidelines sanctioned by the Committee for the Purpose of Control  and  Supervision  of  Experiments  on  Animals  (CPCSEA),  Govt.  of  India (JSSAHER/CPT/IAEC/118/2022).

Diet-induced MASLD model:

After  a  week  of  acclimatization,  the  mice  were  fed  with  either  a  chow  diet  or  a western  diet  with  high  fat,  high  sucrose,  and  high  cholesterol  (WD,  21%  fat,  41% sucrose,  and  1.25%  cholesterol  by  weight,  Research  Diet,  Inc.  USA)  along  with normal water or high sugar (18.9 g/L d-glucose and 23.1 g/L d-fructose) containing drinking water ad libitum for 24 weeks. Mice were continuously monitored for food intake,  water  intake,  and  body  weight.  After  24  weeks,  mice  were  euthanized,  and blood and tissues were collected for further analysis.

In vitro model of steatosis

Huma hepatic cells HepG2 and Huh7 cells were a kind donation from Dr. Suresh Giri, Zydus Cadila, Gujrath, India. HepG2 cells were cultured in DMEM containing 1 g/L glucose,  and  Huh7  cells  were  cultured  in  DMEM  containing  4.5  g/L  glucose.  The culture media were supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum, L-glutamine, and 100 U penicillin-streptomycin and incubated at 37 0 C in 5% CO2.

To develop an in vitro model of steatosis, cells were cultured up to 50% confluency in culture  plates,  followed  by  treatment  with  free  fatty  acids:  0.5  mM  sodium  oleate (SO), 0.5 mM sodium palmitate (SP), or a mixture of both SO and SP (SP+SO) in a 1:1  ratio  in  5%  fatty  acid-free  BSA  containing  culture  media.  After  24  hours  of treatment, the cells were assessed for lipid droplets using Oil Red O staining. For all the  experiments,  cells  were  treated  with  free  fatty  acids  for  24  hours,  and  the  cell pellets were collected for RNA isolation or protein lysate preparation.

AAV expression system

Liver gene expression was modulated using AAV-mediated gene transfer technology as defined previously (29). The siRNA sequences targeting AATF were incorporated into  an  adeno-associated  virus  (AAV)  vector,  under  the  regulation  of  liver-specific thyroxine-binding globulin (TBG) promoter. AAV8-TBG-siAATF and AAV8-TBGnull  constructs  were  generated  by  packaging  into  serotype  8,  titrated  by  Applied Biological Materials Inc. (ABM).

Hepatocyte-specific silencing of AATF in experimental MASH

The mice were fed either a chow diet normal water (CD) or a western diet sugar water (WD).  After  12  weeks  of  dietary  intervention,  mice  were  injected  via  the  tail  vein with  1×10 11 Genome  copies  of  AAV8  serotype  particles  and  randomized  into  four groups:  (i)  CDNW  with  AAV8-TBG-Null,  (ii)  CDNW  with  AAV8-TBG-siAATF, (iii) WDSW with AAV8-TBG-Null, and (iv) WDSW with AAV8-TBG-siAATF. The same dietary regimen was followed until the end of 24 weeks. Throughout the study, mice  were  continuously  monitored  for  food  intake,  water  intake,  and  body  weight. Glucose tolerance test (GTT) and insulin tolerance test (ITT) were performed during the  study  period.  After  24  weeks,  mice  were  euthanized  under  fasting  conditions, followed by blood and tissue collection for further analysis.

Isolation of primary mouse hepatocytes

Primary hepatocytes were isolated from the livers of mice injected with AAV8-TBGNull  and  AAV8-TBG-siAATF  following  previously  established  protocols  (29). Briefly,  mice  were  anesthetized,  and  the  inferior  vena  cava  was  cannulated.  Liver perfusion  was  carried  out  using  pre-warmed  perfusion  buffer  followed  by  the digestion  buffer  containing  25  µg/ml  Liberase.  The  isolated  hepatocytes  were  then filtered through a 70-micron cell strainer and purified via centrifugation in DMEM at 50g for 5 minutes at room temperature. Percoll gradient separation was employed to isolate hepatocytes from nonparenchymal cells. The isolated cells were suspended in DMEM supplemented with  10%  FBS  and  100U  penicillin-streptomycin.  Collagencoated plates were utilized for cell culture in William's E medium.

Glucose and Insulin tolerance tests

Mice were fasted overnight, or 5-6 hours for GTT (glucose tolerance test) and ITT (insulin tolerance test), respectively. The basal level of blood glucose was measured using a glucometer. Further, mice were subjected to oral administration of glucose (1 g/kg body weight) and intraperitoneal injection of insulin (0.75 U/kg body weight). Venous  tail  blood  samples  were  taken  at  different  intervals  between  0  and  120 minutes to measure the blood glucose level.

Biochemical analysis

Blood collected from the fasting mice was centrifuged at 1500 g for 15 min at 4°C. The serum levels of aspartate transaminase (AST), alanine transaminase (ALT), total cholesterol (TC), total triglycerides (TG), and LDL-cholesterol were measured using commercial kits according to the manufacturer's protocol.

Histological analysis

Liver tissues were subjected to formalin fixation and paraffin blocks were prepared. 4µm tissue sections were used for hematoxylin and eosin staining to assess the liver morphology. For pathological grading, stained liver sections were scored by pathologists according to the MASLD activity score comprising the sum of steatosis (0-3),  lobular  inflammation  (0-3),  and  hepatocyte  ballooning  (0-2).  A  cumulative activity score of ≥5 was indicative of MASH.

Oil Red O staining

Oil  Red  O  staining  was  performed  using  frozen  liver  tissues.  Cryosections,  12-μm thick,  were  fixed  with  10%  formaldehyde  for  5  minutes  and  washed  with  distilled water.  Further,  the  slides  were  incubated  with  60%  isopropanol  and  Oil  Red  O solution for one hour. After rinsing off excess stain with distilled water, nuclei were counterstained  with  hematoxylin.  The  slides  were  then  mounted,  and  images  were captured  using  the  Leica  microscope.  For  quantitative  analysis,  the  areas  of  lipid droplets were quantified using ImageJ software.

Picrosirius red staining

The  FFPE  liver  tissue  sections  were  subjected  to  deparaffinization  and  rehydration using  xylene,  followed  by  decreasing  percentages  of  ethanol.  The  slides  were  first stained with hematoxylin for 2 minutes, followed by incubation with the picrosirius red  solution  for  1  hour.  Slides  were  washed  in  0.5%  acidified  water  and  mounted. The images were captured using the Leica microscope and quantified using ImageJ software.

Immunohistochemistry

Immunohistochemistry  was  carried  out  according  to  the  standard  protocol.  Briefly, after deparaffinization and rehydration, FFPE liver tissues were subjected to antigen retrieval with citrate buffer (pH 6) at 94°C for 15 minutes. Following this, the tissue sections were blocked with 3% hydrogen peroxide and normal goat serum for 2 hours. Primary antibodies (AATF at 1:500, F4/80 at 1:100, and Desmin at 1:500 dilutions) were  used  to  stain  the  respective  tissue  sections  overnight  at  4°C  in  a  humidified chamber. The detection of signals was accomplished using the Polyexcel HRP/DAB detection system (PathnSitu, Biotechnologies). Images were captured using the Leica microscope, and quantitative analysis was conducted using ImageJ software.

ELISA

Serum insulin and TNFα levels were quantified using mouse ELISA kits according to the manufacturer's instructions.

Immunoblot analysis

Liver tissues and cell pellets were dissolved in RIPA buffer containing protease/phosphatase inhibitor cocktail. The lysates were obtained by centrifugation at 12000 rpm at 4 0 C for 20 minutes. Protein concentration was estimated by Bradford's protein  estimation  method.  Equivalent  amounts  of  protein  were  resolved  in  SDSPAGE, followed by transfer into the nitrocellulose membrane. Blots were blocked in 5% nonfat dry milk for 1 hour and probed with primary antibodies of interest (antiAATF  1:1000,  anti-β-actin  1:1000,  anti-pJNK,  anti-JNK,  anti-pERK1/2,  and  antiERK1/2  1:100)  overnight  at  4 0 C  and  secondary  antibodies  for  2  hours  at  room temperature. Blots were visualized using Western Bright ECL HRP substrate in the UVitec Alliance Q9 chemiluminescence imaging system and analyzed for densitometric measurements in ImageJ software. The band intensity was normalized with the respective endogenous control.

Quantitative real-time PCR

Total  RNA  was  extracted  from  the  frozen  live  tissues  or  cell  pellets  using  TriZol reagent according to the manufacturer's instructions. RNA was quantified using the nanodrop spectrophotometer and reverse transcribed using the verso cDNA synthesis kit  following  the  manufacturer's  protocol.  Relative  gene  expressions  were  assessed using  quantitative  real-time  PCR  using  the  Rotor-Gene  Q  5plex  HRM  system. DyNamo Colorflash SYBR green kit with 0.5 mM primers (IDT), and 50 ng of cDNA in  a  20  μl  reaction  volume  were  used  to  set  up  the  reaction.  The  mRNA  levels  of selected  genes  were  calculated  after  normalization  to  β-actin  by  using  the  2 (-ΔΔC(T)) method. Primer sequences are provided in Supplementary Table 1.

Untargeted metabolomics

i. Metabolite extraction and LC-MS analysis:

Metabolites were extracted following previously established protocols (30). Briefly, liver  tissues  were  lyophilized in  a  vacuum freeze dryer  for 24 hours, and 50 mg of samples were homogenized with 50 mM ammonium bicarbonate (3:1 w/v). Samples were extracted in cold methanol by centrifugation at 18,000× g for 15 minutes at 4°C. Supernatants  are  dried  and  reconstituted  in  MeOH:  water  (3:1  v/v)  for  LC/MS analysis.  The  hepatic  metabolome  was  analyzed  using  a  Vanquish  UHPLC  system (Thermo Fisher) coupled with an Orbitrap Eclipse Tribrid mass spectrometer (Thermo Fisher).  Samples  were  separated  on  an  RP  column,  2.1  x  150  mm,  1.8  um  particle size,  and  0.3  mL/min  flow  rate  with  two  eluents  for  positive  and  negative  polarity modes.

ii. Metabolomics data analysis

The data processing was done using the Compound Discoverer 3.3 software (Thermo Fisher Scientific). A pre-existing workflow template titled 'Untargeted Metabolomics with Statistics Detect Unknowns with ID using Online Databases and mzLogic' was used  to  identify  the  differences  between  samples.  For  identification,  the  mass tolerance  was  5  ppm,  and  the  retention  time  tolerance  was  0.2  min.  The  minimum peak  intensity  was  10,000,  and  the  chromatographic  S/N  threshold  was  1.5.  The solvent blanks were used to create exclusion lists, mark background components, and filter  the  background  components  from  the  results  table.  A  pooled  quality  control (QC) sample was used for compound identification only.

To  visualize  the  similarity  between  the  metabolomic  profiles  of  the  samples  from different  groups,  principal  component  analysis  (PCA)  and  heatmap  plots  were generated.  All  the  analyses  were  conducted  based  on  the  compound's  peak  areas. Differential metabolite analysis was conducted using a Student's t test, considering p value<0.05  as  significant  and  log2  foldchange  >=  |1|  as  either  an  upregulated  or downregulated metabolite. The log2 foldchange per metabolite was calculated as ratio between group areas, transformed to log2 scale, with group area being calculated as the median of compound area per sample within the group. The up- or down-regulated metabolites were visualized using volcano plots. P-values were adjusted for multiple testing using the Benjamini Hochberg correction for false discovery rate.

We  applied  the  GSEA  algorithm  against  the  Kyoto  Encyclopedia  of  Genes  and Genomes (KEGG) Mus musculus (House Mouse) pathway library, lipid  superclass and subclass. Significant pathways (p<0.05) from the GSEA method were analyzed further. Pathway enrichment against the small molecule pathway database (SMPDB) database  was  performed  separately  for  upregulated  and  downregulated  metabolites using MetaboAnalyst 6.0. For overlaying the regulation of metabolites involved in the glycerophospholipid  metabolism  pathway,  the  compound  list  and  pathway  were accessed from the KEGG database and visualized using the Pathview R package. In order to visualize the metabolic network, we used MetScape (http://metscape.ncibi.org./), the Cytoscape plugin to identify the interaction between the metabolites involved in the glycerophospholipid metabolism pathway.

Statistical Analysis

Statistical  analyses  were  performed  using  GraphPad  Prism  6.0  software  (GraphPad, Inc., La Jolla, CA, USA). Data were represented as the mean ± standard error of the mean. Statistical  significance  was  analyzed  using  Student's  t-test  for  two  groups  or analysis  of  variance  (ANOVA)  with  post  hoc  Bonferroni  correction  for  multiple comparisons and p values < 0.05 (*/ # ) or < 0.001 (**/ ## ) were considered significant.

RESULTS

AATF  is  highly  expressed  in  experimental  models  of  metabolic  dysfunctionassociated steatotic liver disease

We initially determined the hepatic AATF expression in the cellular model and also across different stages of MASLD in an in vivo murine model. Free fatty acids, such as sodium palmitate (PA) or sodium oleate (SO), were used to mimic liver steatosis in the human  hepatic cell lines HepG2  and  Huh7.  Upon  confirmation  of  lipid accumulation as visualized by Oil Red O staining ( Figure 1A ), AATF expression was determined.  Our  observations  revealed  a  significant  upregulation  of  AATF  both  at mRNA  and  protein  levels  in  the in  vitro model.  Furthermore,  we  employed  a preclinical model of MASLD, which developed MAFL at 12 weeks and MASH at 24 weeks in western diet sugar water  mice  compared to  chow diet  normal water  mice ( Figure 1E ).  AATF mRNA and protein levels were notably elevated at MAFL and MASH stages compared to the control ( Figure 1F and 1G ). These collective findings underscore  the  upregulation  of  AATF  during  the  progressive  stages  of  MASLD, implicating its potential significance in disease progression.

Silencing  of  hepatic  AATF  prevents  WDSW-induced  steatosis,  steatohepatitis, and fibrosis

To assess the effect of AATF on liver metabolism, we silenced AATF expression in the hepatocytes of mice using liver-directed AAV8 under the control of the thyroxinebinding  globulin  (TBG)-mediated  gene  silencing  system.  Mice  were  fed  chow  diet normal  water  (CD)  or  western  diet  sugar  water  (WD).  After  12  weeks,  all  mice received tail vein injections of either AAV8-TBG-Null or AAV8-TBG-siAATF and were monitored  for  an  additional  12  weeks  ( Figure  2A ).  The  expression  of  AATF was  validated  in  various  tissues  of  mice,  such  as  the  liver,  adipose  tissue,  heart, kidney, pancreas, and spleen. We found reduced AATF expression only in the liver tissue of AAV8-TBG-siAATF mice compared to AAV8-TBG-Null mice ( Figure 2B and  2C ).  Additionally,  we  confirmed  AATF  silencing  specifically  in  hepatocytes, with no effect on non-parenchymal cells ( Figure 2D ).

Hepatic  AATF  silencing  for  12  weeks  significantly  reduced  body  weight,  liver weight, liver-body ratio, and body fat mass compared to those treated with AAV8TBG-Null  in  WD  mice  but  had  no  effect  in  CD  mice  ( Figure  2E-  2H ).  AATF silencing also improved glucose tolerance and insulin sensitivity, as demonstrated by the glucose tolerance test (GTT) and the insulin tolerance test (ITT), respectively, in WD mice ( Figure 2I and 2J ). Notably, WD siAATF mice exhibited decreased blood glucose levels,  fasting  insulin  levels,  and  homeostatic  model  assessment  for  insulin resistance (HOMA-IR) compared to WD control mice, indicating alleviation of highfat and high sugar diet-induced glucose metabolism  exacerbation and insulin resistance  ( Figure  3A-  3C ).  Furthermore,  hepatic  AATF  silencing  led  to  notable reductions  in  serum  levels  of  ALT,  AST,  total  cholesterol,  triglycerides,  and  LDLcholesterol in WD mice compared to WD controls ( Figure 3D- 3H ).  No significant differences were observed in CD mice treated with AAV8-TBG-siAATF compared to AAV8-TBG-Null mice. Macroscopic examination revealed healthier liver morphology in WD siAATF mice compared to WD controls ( Figure 3I ). Histological analysis of liver sections showed decreased steatosis and substantial improvement in steatohepatitis  (MASH)  upon  AATF  silencing  compared  to  controls  in  WD  mice ( Figure 3J ). Similar results were obtained with Oil Red O staining of liver sections, where there was reduced intracellular lipid droplet accumulation in WD siAATF mice compared  to  WD  controls  ( Figure  3K ),  indicating  that  hepatic  AATF  inhibition improves  metabolic  health  by  ameliorating  liver  injury  and dysregulated  lipid metabolism.

Consistent with these findings, histological scoring for steatosis, lobular inflammation, and hepatocyte ballooning was reduced in hepatic AATF-silenced WD mice,  with  an  improvement  in  MASLD  activity  score  compared  to  WD  controls ( Figure 4A- 4D ).  More interestingly, we observed reduced fibrosis in WD siAATF mice compared to WD controls, as evidenced by picrosirius red staining ( Figure 4E ). In addition, hepatic stellate cell activation marked by desmin and  α-SMA overexpression was attenuated in WD siAATF mice ( Figure 4F and 4G ).  The  key markers for liver fibrosis, including Col1A1, Col3A1, and TGF-β, were downregulated  in  WD  siAATF  mice,  suggesting  that  AATF  silencing  reduces fibrogenic activation in WD mice ( Figure 4H- 4J ).

AATF silencing alleviates hepatic ER stress and inflammation in Experimental MASH

Endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress plays a prime role in the pathogenesis of MASH by  promoting  hepatic  inflammation  and  fibrosis.  Several  factors  contribute  to  ER stress,  including  lipid  accumulation  in  hepatocytes,  mitochondrial  dysfunction,  and oxidative stress (31). In our investigation, we explored the impact of hepatic AATF silencing on the expression of key ER stress markers. Remarkably, in WD siAATF mice,  we  found  significant  downregulation  in  the  mRNA  levels  of  CHOP,  Grp78, PERK, and ATF4 compared to WD controls ( Figure 5A- 5D).

Furthermore, elucidating the intricate crosstalk between ER stress and inflammation, our study revealed that ER stress activation triggers inflammatory signaling pathways, notably  stress  kinases,  leading  to  the  release  of  pro-inflammatory  cytokines  in  WD control mice. This increase in inflammation was remarkably reduced by the silencing of  hepatic  AATF  in  WD  mice,  as  evidenced  by  the  reduced  expression  of  proinflammatory  cytokines  IL-6,  IL-1β,  and  TNF-α  ( Figure  5E-  5H ).  Concurrently, phosphorylation of JNK and Erk1/2 was noticeably diminished in WD siAATF mice compared to WD controls ( Figure  5I  and  5J ).  The  immunostaining for the mouse macrophage marker F4/80 corroborated these findings, showing a marked reduction in  response to AATF knockdown in WD mice ( Figure 5K ).  These  findings  signify AATF as an important mediator for ER stress-induced hepatic inflammation.

Effect of hepatic AATF silencing on metabolic profiles in Experimental MASH

Hepatic  metabolite  profiling  was  conducted  through  an  untargeted  metabolomics analysis, where raw mass spectra peaks were meticulously annotated using mzCloud (ddMS2)  and  ChemSpider  (formula  or  exact  mass).  PCA  and  PLS-DA  techniques were  utilized  to  discern  differences  between  experimental  groups  and  identify  the variations  that  contribute  to  these  classifications.  Notably,  there  was  significant separation of liver metabolites between the WD siAATF and WD control groups, as well  as  significant  separation  between  the  WD  control  and  CD  control  groups,  as depicted by both the Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and PLS-DA plots ( Figure 6A  and  6B). Further,  unsupervised  hierarchical  clustering  analysis  of  differentially expressed metabolites (DEMs) revealed a distinct pattern between WD siAATF and WD control (WDC) in contrast to CD siAATF and CD control (CDC) (Figure 6C) . Univariate analysis identified 487 metabolites altered in WD siAATF, of which 412 were upregulated in contrast to 197 upregulated metabolites in WD control. Similarly, 867 metabolites were downregulated in WD  control mice, whereas only 75 metabolites  were  downregulated  in  WD  siAATF  mice,  clearly  showing  marked differences in the metabolome profiling upon hepatic AATF silencing. Subsequently, data underwent normalization and filtering based on significant differential metabolite analysis  between  groups  (FC  >1  and  <1,  P<0.05  were  considered  significant). Consistently,  the  volcano  plots  also  showed  a  similar  distribution  of  differentially altered metabolites between WD control vs. CD control (Figure 6E) and WD siAATF vs. WD control (Figure 6F).

Identification of differentially expressed metabolites between WD siAATF and WD control samples

To comprehend the significance of the dysregulated metabolites in relation to specific biological  processes,  a  metabolic  pathway  analysis  was  conducted  using  the  online tool MetaboAnalyst 6.0. This analysis included all metabolites that were found to be significant (p < 0.05). The enrichment analysis between WD control and CD control ( Figure 7A ) revealed metabolic processes involved in the onset of MASH, including gluconeogenesis, bile acid synthesis, pyrimidine metabolism, and steroidogenesis. On the other hand, KEGG pathway analysis revealed that 74 pathways were enriched by the DEMs in the WD siAATF vs. WD control groups. Of these, glycerophospholipid metabolism, ether lipid metabolism, nicotinamide metabolism, linoleic acid metabolism,  riboflavin  metabolism,  and  pyruvate  metabolism  were  significantly upregulated upon hepatic silencing of AATF. In contrast, branched-chain amino acid biosynthesis,  sulfur  metabolism,  and  glycerolipid  metabolism  were  downregulated (Figure 7B) Further, DEMs were enriched against lipid superclass within the KEGG database, which unveiled the upregulation of glycerophospholipids (glycerophosphocholines, glycerophosphoserines, and glycerophosphoethanolamines),  fatty  esters,  and  eicosanoids  in  WD  siAATF  mice (Figure 7C). Delving deeper into lipid subclass analysis, phosphatidylcholine (PC), phosphatidylserine  (PS),  phosphatidyl  ethanolamine  (PE),  lysoPC,  lysoPE,  lysoPS, and  vitamin  D3  were  elevated  in  WD  siAATF  mice (Figure  7D). Of  particular interest, DEMs involved  in  glycerophospholipid  metabolism  were  represented  as  a heatmap (Figure 7E).

A  network  analysis  of  putative  interacting  proteins  of  the  metabolites  significantly altered by hepatic silencing of AATF in WD mice was identified and enriched in the glycerophospholipid  metabolism  pathway  in  the  KEGG  database (Figure  8A) .  Of particular importance, levels of phosphocholine, phosphatidylcholine (PC), lysophophatidylethanolamine  (LPE),  and  lysophosphatidylcholine  (LPC)  were  all significantly increased in WD siAATF mice compared to WD controls ( Figure 8C) . To gain deeper insights into the biological functions of the differentiated metabolites, a functional relationship network was constructed utilizing Metscape. The resulting network  revealed  that  most  of  the  metabolites  were  primarily  involved  in  the metabolism  of  glycerophospholipids,  linoleate,  and  phosphatidyl  inositol (Figure 8C). Collectively, these findings underscore the profound impact of AATF silencing on liver metabolites, particularly in the upregulation of glycerophospholipid biosynthesis, notably in PC and PE species.

Beta-oxidation  of  fatty  acids  is  intricately  linked  to  the  pathogenesis  of  MASH. Dysregulation of beta-oxidation pathways contributes to hepatic lipid accumulation, oxidative stress, and inflammation, driving the progression of the disease (32). Along similar  lines,  our  studies  revealed  the  upregulation  of  metabolites  involved  in mitochondrial beta-oxidation pathway alongside the biosynthesis of phosphatidylcholine (PC) in WD siAATF through pathway enrichment analysis using the  SMPB  database  ( Figure  9A ).  Consistently,  metabolites  involved  in  MASH development pathways were downregulated in WD siAATF mice ( Figure 9B) . Thus, we hypothesized that AATF silencing promotes beta-oxidation of fatty acids, thereby ameliorating MASH. In accordance with our hypothesis, our findings revealed notable upregulation  of  key  genes  associated  with  the  beta-oxidation  pathway,  including carnitine  palmitoyltransferase-1  alpha  (CPT1α),  a  pivotal  enzyme  facilitating  fatty acid transport into mitochondria, and the regulators of beta-oxidation, peroxisome proliferator-activated receptors alpha and gamma (PPAR-α and PPAR-γ), in  WD  siAATF  mice  compared  to  controls.  Additionally,  the  expression  levels  of peroxisome  proliferator-activated  receptor  gamma  coactivator  1-alpha  (PGC1α),  a coactivator of PPAR-α and PPAR-γ, were found to be elevated in WD siAATF mice, indicative  of  enhanced  mitochondrial  fatty  acid  uptake  and  oxidation  ( Figure  9C ). Collectively,  hepatic  silencing  of  AATF  ameliorates  MASH  by  promoting  βoxidation of fatty acids ( Figure 9D ).

DISCUSSION

Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD), commonly known as non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), is a multifactorial condition influenced by various molecular drivers (5). Dysregulation of lipid metabolism is a hallmark of MASLD. Increased influx of free fatty acids (FFAs) from adipose tissue and dietary sources, along with impaired fatty acid oxidation and export of triglycerides from the liver,  contribute  to  hepatic  steatosis  (10). Insulin  resistance  in  hepatocytes  leads  to impaired suppression of  gluconeogenesis and increased lipogenesis,  promoting hepatic lipid accumulation (33). Several factors associated with metabolic dysfunction, including lipid accumulation, oxidative stress, and insulin resistance, can trigger ER stress in hepatocytes (31). Inflammatory pathways play a crucial role in the progression of MASLD from simple steatosis to MASH. Activation of inflammatory signaling  pathways  leads  to  the  production  of  pro-inflammatory  cytokines  and chemokines, contributing to hepatocyte injury and fibrosis (34). Thus, identifying and understanding the molecular drivers involved in MASLD pathogenesis is essential for developing targeted therapeutic strategies aimed at preventing disease progression and improving clinical outcomes for patients with MASLD. The evolving understanding of the disease underscores the urgent need for new therapeutic targets. The complex pathophysiology of MASLD involves multiple metabolic, inflammatory, and fibrotic pathways, making it essential to identify novel targets that can address these different aspects. Advanced molecular profiling techniques, including transcriptomics, proteomics,  metabolomics,  and  epigenomics,  can  provide  valuable  insights  into  the underlying mechanisms driving MASLD and guide the development of personalized treatment approaches.

AATF has been implicated in the pathogenesis of several cancers due to its role in the modulation of apoptotic pathways (19). We, for the first time, elucidated how TNF-α upregulates the expression of AATF via SREBP-1 and promotes hepatocarcinogenesis  (35).  Further  studies  revealed  the  role  of  AATF  in  impeding HCC angiogenesis by downregulating PEDF (24). Recently, we investigated the role of AATF in MASH-HCC and showed that TACE inhibition by Marimastat prevents AATF-mediated inflammation, fibrosis,  and  oncogenesis  (27).  In  the  current  study, we  identified  for  the  first  time  the  role  of  AATF  in  lipid  metabolism  and  its implications in the development of MASH. The preclinical murine model of MASH used in this study is C57BL/6 mice fed with chow diet normal water and western diet sugar  water  that  recapitulates  the  features  of  metabolic  syndrome  and  MASH  with progressive fibrosis, unlike the DIAMOND mouse model, which is a cross between two mouse  strains (C57Bl/6J and 129S1/SvImJ) (36). The mice exhibit the development of fatty liver in 12 weeks after starting a western diet supplemented with sugar water, advancing to MASH and fibrosis at 24 weeks. Since AATF was found to be  upregulated  in  both  cellular  and  preclinical  models  of  MASH,  we  evaluated  the effect  of  liver-specific  silencing  of  AATF  against  hepatic  lipid  accumulation  in experimental  MASH  and  its  influence  on  the  genes  and  proteins  linked  to  the biological processes of MASH development and progression. The silencing of hepatic AATF showed a significant  reduction  in  body  weight  and  liver  weight  in  WDSW mice and had no effect on CDNW mice. Insulin resistance was notably improved, and we  also  observed  substantial  improvement  in  both  histological  and  biochemical parameters in WD siAATF mice compared to WDSW controls. Consistent with these data,  AATF  silencing  also  reduced  fibrosis  in  the  WDSW  mice.  These  findings highlight the potential function of AATF in MASLD progression and set the stage for a deep dive into molecular insights.

This is the first study to have the hepatic metabolome performed in mice silenced with AATF. The mechanism by which AATF ameliorates MASH is elusive, and therefore high  attention  was  paid  to  identifying  the  alterations  in  liver  metabolites  in  the experimental  MASH,  where  AATF  was  silenced  versus  the  control  by  untargeted metabolomics.  It  was  interesting  to  note  the  upregulation  of  glycerophospholipids, particularly phosphatidylcholines (PC), phosphatidylserines (PS), and phosphatidylethanolamines  (PE),  in  the  WD  siAATF  mice.  Additionally,  their breakdown  products  like  LysoPC,  LysoPS,  and  LysoPE  were  also  increased.  The metabolite-metabolite interaction network highlighted the potential functional relationships of glycerophospholipid with linoleate metabolism. It is noteworthy that alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), an essential omega-3 fatty acid, is largely attributed to its anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and lipid-lowering properties in MASLD (37). ALA

can  influence  lipid  metabolism  by  modulating  the  expression  of  genes  involved  in fatty acid oxidation and lipid synthesis. It also promotes the activation of peroxisome proliferator-activated receptors (PPARs), which play a crucial role in lipid metabolism (38).

Previous  studies  have  also  reported  alterations  in  phospholipid  content  in  the  liver biopsies  of  NASH  patients,  suggesting  that  impaired  phospholipid  synthesis  is associated  with  disease  progression  (13,14). The  low  hepatic  phosphatidylcholine (PC) levels and the altered hepatic PC to phosphatidylethanolamine (PE) ratio appear to play  significant  roles  in  the  development  of  MASLD  (39).  However,  the pathophysiology  of  these  lipid-induced  processes  remains  not  fully  understood. Moreover,  low  PC  levels  have  previously  been  shown  to  activate  sterol  regulatory element-binding  protein  1  (SREBP1)  (40). Essential  phospholipids  (EPLs)  rich  in phosphatidylcholine (PCH) are a widely used treatment option for fatty liver disease. There  is  substantial  and  consistent  clinical  evidence  indicating  that  treatment  with EPLs leads to the regression of steatosis (41). Studies show that phosphatidylcholine metabolism  intersects  with  various  metabolic  pathways,  and  optimizing  PC  levels could  potentially  help  regulate  lipid  metabolism  and  insulin  sensitivity,  thereby mitigating MASLD  progression (42). In addition, PC may have antioxidant properties, which could counteract oxidative damage in the liver and protect against disease progression (43). Several classes of glycerophospholipids, such as PC, PE, PS, and  PI,  are  implicated  in  MASLD  progression,  where  dysregulation  of  these glycerophospholipids is linked with insulin, inflammation, and fibrosis, which are the hallmarks  of  MASLD  (44).    Consistent  with  this  observation,  we  found  that  the markers of ER stress and inflammation were significantly reduced in AATF-silenced WD mice.  Additionally,  the  liver  metabolite  profile  in  WD  siAATF  mice  showed decreased branched-chain amino acid metabolism and sulfur metabolism involved in the MASH pathogenesis in contrast to increased pyruvate metabolism, NAD and FAD metabolism, as well as one-carbon metabolic pathways.

An  intriguing  observation  in  the  current  study  using  pathway  enrichment  analysis showed the mitochondrial beta-oxidation of the fatty acid pathway in the upregulated category along with phosphatidylcholine biosynthesis in WD  siAATF  mice. Corroborating  these  results,  studies  by  Maev  et  al.,  have  provided  evidence  that phosphatidylcholine  supplementation  may  help  improve  liver  function  and  reduce liver fat accumulation in fatty liver disease, potentially through its ability to activate PPARs  and  subsequent  upregulation  of  PPAR-dependent  enzymes,  including  acyl CoA oxidase and carnitine palmitoyl transferase (CPT), modulating lipid metabolism (45). Beta-oxidation helps to efficiently metabolize fatty acids, and thereby enhanced beta-oxidation may alleviate inflammation and oxidative stress in the liver, which are key drivers of MASLD progression (46). Several genes, such as CPT1 and PPARs, encode enzymes involved in beta-oxidation, and henceforth, upregulated expression of these genes would enhance beta-oxidation capacity, potentially improving metabolic health (47,48). Consistent with this, our study also showed the upregulation of CPT1, PPARα, and PPARγ genes in the liver tissue of WD siAATF compared to WD controls.

Despite the extensive studies on MASLD, there is a need to understand the interplay between  the  molecular  drivers  involved  in  lipid  metabolism,  insulin  resistance, oxidative  stress,  and  inflammation  that  contribute  to  the  pathogenesis  of  MASLD.

This study offers insight for the first time on the involvement of AATF in fatty acid beta-oxidation, which plays a crucial role in the regulation of lipid metabolism and energy homeostasis in the liver. Thus, AATF is potentially a promising therapeutic target for the treatment of MASLD. Furthermore, studies are focused on elucidating the downstream interacting partners of AATF in fatty acid oxidation. An integrated transcriptomic and metabolomic analysis would aid in a better understanding of the role of AATF in the regulation of hepatic lipid metabolism.

FIGURE LEGENDS

Figure 1. AATF is upregulated in the in vitro and in vivo MASH models

HepG2 and Huh7 cells were treated with 0.5mM of SP, SO, or SP+SO for 24 hours. Lipid  accumulation  was  determined  by  Oil  Red  O  staining  (A).  The  mRNA expression of AATF in HepG2 (B) and Huh7 (C) cells.  Protein levels of AATF in HepG2 and Huh7 cells (D). Diet-induced MASH model was developed by feeding the mice with chow diet normal water (CDNW) or western diet sugar water (WDSW) for 24 weeks. Representative H&E images and AATF immunohistochemistry of control, MAFL, and MASH stages (E). hepatic AATF mRNA (F) and protein (G) levels were evaluated in mice liver tissue. All the data are expressed as mean±SEM for 6-8 mice per group. Statistical significance was analyzed by a student's t-test. **p<0.001 or *P < 0.05 compared to normal or control.

Figure 2. Hepatocyte-specific knockdown of AATF improves metabolic health

C57Bl/6 mice were fed chow diet normal water or western diet sugar water for 12 weeks. Liver-specific AAV8-TBG-Null or AAV8-TBG-siAATF vectors were injected via tail vein into mice and monitored for an additional 12 weeks (A). Tissue distribution  of  vector  (B),  liver-specific  (C),  and  hepatocyte-specific  (D)  of  AATF silencing  were  determined.  Body weight (E), liver weight (F), liver-body ratio (G), and adipose tissue weight (H) were determined. Glucose tolerance test (I) and insulin tolerance test (J) were performed. All the data are expressed as mean±SEM for 6-8 mice per group. Statistical significance was analyzed by a student's t-test. ## p<0.001 compared to CD control; **p<0.001 compared to WD control or AAV8 control.

Figure  3.  Hepatocyte-specific  knockdown  of  AATF  improves  liver  injury  and impaired lipid metabolism

Mice were euthanized after 24 weeks and blood was collected. Fasting blood glucose (A), fasting insulin (B), and HOMA-IR (C) were determined. Serum levels of ALT (D), AST (E), total cholesterol (F), triglycerides (G), and LDL-cholesterol (H) were measured.  Representative  gross  liver  images  (I),  H&E  staining  (J),  and  Oil  Red  O staining (K). Data expressed as mean ±SEM for 6-8 mice. Statistical significance was analyzed  by  a  student's  t-test. ## p<0.001  compared  to  CD  control;  **p<0.001 compared to WD control.

Figure 4. Silencing of AATF ameliorates MASH and liver fibrosis

Histology scores were calculated for mice liver sections. Representative images and scoring  of  steatosis  (A),  lobular  inflammation  (B),  hepatocyte  ballooning  (C),  and MASLD  activity  score  (D).  Representative  images  of  picrosirius  red  staining  and fibrosis  scoring  (E),  immunostaining  of  desmin  with  scoring  (F).  Hepatic  mRNA expression of Col1A1 (G), Col3A1 (H), α-SMA (I), and TGF-β (J) quantified. Data expressed  as  mean  ±SEM  for  6-8  mice.  Statistical  significance  was  analyzed  by  a student's t-test. ## p<0.001 or # p<0.05 compared to CD control; **p<0.001 compared to WD control.

Figure 5. Effect of silencing AATF  on  ER  stress and Inflammation in Experimental MASH

C57Bl/6  mice  were  fed  chow  diet  normal  water  (CD)  or  western  diet  sugar  water (WD) for 12 weeks. Liver-specific AAV8-TBG-Null or AAV8-TBG-siAATF vectors were injected via tail vein into mice and monitored for an additional 12 weeks. (A-D) hepatic mRNA expression of ER stress markers- CHOP, Grp78, PERK, and ATF4, (E-G) hepatic mRNA expression of inflammatory markers- IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α, (H)  serum  TNF-  α  by  ELISA.  (I  and  J)  Liver  tissue  lysates  were  used  for immunoblotting of inflammatory markers- pJNK and pERK1/2. (K) F4/80 staining. Data expressed as mean ±SEM for 6-8 mice. Statistical significance was analyzed by a  student's  t-test. ## p<0.001  or # p<0.05  compared  to  CD  control;  **p<0.001  or *p<0.05 compared to WD control.

Figure  6.  Effect  of  AATF  silencing  on  hepatic  metabolites  in  experimental MASH

C57Bl/6  mice  were  fed  chow  diet  normal  water  (CD)  or  western  diet  sugar  water (WD)  for  12  weeks.  Liver-specific  AAV8-TBG-blank  (C)  or  AAV8-TBG-siAATF (siAATF) vectors were injected via tail vein into mice and monitored for an additional 12  weeks.  After  mice  were  euthanized,  liver  tissues  were  collected  for  untargeted metabolomic  study.  (A) PCA  score  plot  (B)  PLS-DA  plot  among  the  four  groups showing a clear group separation between the groups, (C) Classification of differential metabolites  between  the  four  groups,  (D)  Venn  diagram  showing  the  up  and downregulated metabolites between the WDC vs. CDC and WDsiAATF vs. WDC. Volcano plot for differential metabolites in WDC vs. CDC (E) and WDsiAATF vs. WDC (F).

Figure 7. Hepatic silencing of AATF increases glycerophospholipid synthesis in MASH mice

C57Bl/6  mice  were  fed  chow  diet  normal  water  (CD)  or  western  diet  sugar  water (WD)  for  12  weeks.  Liver-specific  AAV8-TBG-Null  (C)  or  AAV8-TBG-siAATF (siAATF) vectors were injected via tail vein into mice and monitored for an additional 12 weeks. Untargeted metabolomics was carried out in mice liver tissues. Bubble plot showing top 20 KEGG pathways enrichment using the mummichog GSEA algorithm in  (A)  WDC vs. CDC and (B) WDC vs. WDsiAATF. A bubble plot showing lipid superclass  (C)  and  lipid  subclass  (D)  enrichment  using  the  mummichog  GSEA algorithm.  (E)  Heat  map  of  different  glycerophospholipids  differentially  altered between WDC and WDsiAATF mice.

Figure 8. Metabolic networking of glycerophospholipid metabolism

Untargeted  metabolomics  was  carried  out  in  mice  liver  tissues.  Metabolites  of glycerophospholipid  metabolism  were  analyzed  and  functional  correlation  network analysis was carried out. (A) Glycerophospholipid metabolism pathway visualized in pathview  network  highlighting  the  dysregulated  compounds  (up/downregulated)  in WDsiAATF vs. WDC. (B) Relative abundance of phosphocholine, PC (phosphatidylcholine), LPE (lysophophatidylethanolamine), and LPC (lysophosphatidylcholine). (C) Metabolite functional interaction network of glycerophospholipid metabolism pathway. Statistical significance was analyzed by a student's t-test. **p<0.001 or *p<0.05 compared to WDC.

Figure 9. AATF knockdown promotes β oxidation of fatty acids in MASH mice

Untargeted metabolomics was carried out in mice liver tissues. Pathway analysis and enrichment analysis of metabolomic data were carried out. Enrichment analysis based on the Small Molecule Pathway Database (SMPDB) was performed (A) Up regulated pathways (B) downregulated pathways. (C) hepatic mRNA expression of mitochondrial  β  oxidation  genes  (CPT-1α,  PGC-1α,  PPAR-α,  and  PPAR-γ).  Data expressed as mean ±SEM. Statistical significance was analyzed by a student's t-test. **P < 0.001 or *P < 0.05 compared to WDC. (D) Schematic representation depicting how hepatic silencing of AATF ameliorates MASH by promoting β oxidation of fatty acids.

metadata.preprint_title: Silencing of Hepatic AATF Ameliorates Metabolic Dysfunction Associated Steatohepatitis by Promoting Fatty Acid β-Oxidation in Experimental models

pub_metadata.preprint_title: Silencing of Hepatic AATF Ameliorates Metabolic Dysfunction Associated Steatohepatitis by Promoting Fatty Acid β-Oxidation in Experimental models
pub_metadata.preprint_authors: N. Srinivas, A.; Suresh, D.; Moorthy, M.; Ramaswamy, G.; Santhekadur, P. K.; Suvarna, D.; Kumar, D. P.
pub_metadata.preprint_abstract:
Background & aimsMetabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH) is a multifactorial disease driven by complex molecular mechanisms. Identifying key regulators is critical for developing targeted therapies. Here, we demonstrate the impact of the loss of apoptosis antagonizing transcription factor (AATF) on hepatic lipid metabolism and MASH progression.

MethodsA preclinical mouse model recapitulating human MASH was established by feeding C57Bl/6 mice either a chow diet (CD) or a western diet with sugar water (WD). Hepatic AATF silencing was achieved by tail vein injection of siAATF delivered by adeno-associated virus 8 (AAV8) with the liver-specific thyroxine-binding globulin (TBG) promoter. In addition to histological, biochemical, and molecular biology evaluations, the mechanistic insights were derived from whole transcriptomics and untargeted metabolomics analyses.

ResultsAAV8-mediated specific knockdown of AATF in hepatocytes significantly reduced body weight, liver weight, and insulin resistance in mice fed with western diet (WD). However, no such effects were observed in mice fed with a chow diet (CD). Further analyses showed reduced liver injury, steatosis, and steatohepatitis in WDsiAATF mice. Transcriptomic analysis demonstrated that loss of AATF alleviated cellular stress, inflammation, and fibrosis in WD-fed mice. Moreover, AATF silencing led to alterations in lipid metabolism, notably by decreasing hepatic lipogenesis in the WD mice. Interestingly, untargeted metabolomics revealed an increase in the biosynthesis of glycerophospholipids and beta-oxidation of fatty acids in WDsiAATF mice.

ConclusionOur findings reveal a previously unrecognized role of AATF as a central regulator of hepatic lipid metabolism in MASH, acting through the AKT-mTORC1 signaling pathway, and establish its inhibition as a promising therapeutic strategy for metabolic liver disease.
